python.note: Hash starts a line comment to EOL.

lua.note: Double-hyphen line comment.

c.note: // supported since C99; /* */ also works.

rust.note: /// is a doc comment.

zig.note: /// is a doc comment; no block comments exist.

python.note: No true block comment; triple-quoted strings '''...''' are often used as a substitute but are string literals.

ruby.note: =begin/=end must start at column 0 on their own lines.

lua.note: Long-bracket block comment; --[==[ ]==] for nesting.

c.note: Cannot be nested in C.

rust.note: Block comments can nest in Rust.

zig.note: Zig deliberately has no block comments; only // line comments.

haskell.note: Nestable.

python.note: Newline ends a statement; ';' is optional separator.

ruby.note: Newline ends a statement; ';' optional separator.

javascript.note: Semicolon; optional via ASI but canonical to use.

typescript.note: Semicolon; ASI applies as in JS.

lua.note: Statements separated by newline; ; optional/allowed.

c.note: Semicolon terminates statements.

rust.note: Semicolon ends statements; trailing expression without ; is the block value.

go.note: Semicolons auto-inserted by lexer; newlines terminate statements.

swift.note: Semicolons optional; required only to separate multiple statements on one line.

julia.note: Newline ends statement; ; optional/suppresses REPL output and separates inline statements.

haskell.note: Layout/indentation-sensitive; no terminator. (; and {} optional in explicit-layout blocks like do.)

python.note: All names are rebindable; no declaration keyword.

php.note: Variables start with $; no type declaration needed.

javascript.note: let is the modern mutable binding.

typescript.note: Type inferred as number; let x: number = 1 to annotate.

lua.note: local declares a mutable local variable.

c.note: Variables are mutable by default.

rust.note: let bindings are immutable unless marked mut.

zig.note: var is mutable; type may be inferred as 'var x = @as(i32, 1);'.

go.note: Or var x = 1 / var x int = 1.

haskell.note: Pure bindings are immutable; true mutable cell needs IORef/STRef in a monad. let x = 1 is an immutable binding.

python.note: No real constants; UPPERCASE is convention only. typing.Final is a hint, not enforced.

ruby.note: Capitalized name is a constant; reassignment only warns, not enforced.

php.note: const at compile time; define('X', 1) at runtime. No $ on constants.

javascript.note: const binding is immutable (binding, not deep).

lua.note: <const> attribute (Lua 5.4+); earlier Lua has no const.

c.note: const qualifier; not always a true compile-time constant (a const int is not a constant expression usable in array bounds at block scope).

cpp.note: constexpr int x = 1; for compile-time constants.

rust.note: Immutable let binding; const X: i32 = 1; for a true compile-time constant.

zig.note: const is an immutable binding; comptime by default for literals.

csharp.note: const for compile-time; readonly for fields

kotlin.note: val = read-only; const val only for compile-time top-level/object

julia.note: const binds type/identity; not a deep-immutable value.

haskell.note: Top-level/let binding; immutable by default.

lua.note: Integer subtype in Lua 5.3+; just number before.

rust.note: Defaults to i32; suffixes like 42u64.

zig.note: Untyped comptime_int until coerced.

lua.note: Float subtype.

c.note: double by default; 3.14f for float.

rust.note: Defaults to f64.

zig.note: Untyped comptime_float until coerced.

csharp.note: double by default; 3.14f for float, 3.14m for decimal

ruby.note: Double quotes allow interpolation/escapes; single quotes are literal.

php.note: Double quotes interpolate; single quotes are literal.

javascript.note: Single or double quotes both valid.

lua.note: Double or single quotes; [[...]] for long strings.

c.note: char array (NUL-terminated).

cpp.note: const char[]; "hi"s gives std::string with using namespace std::string_literals.

rust.note: &str slice; String::from("hi") for owned.

zig.note: Type *const [2:0]u8 (NUL-terminated byte array pointer).

julia.note: Double quotes; single quotes are Char.

lua.note: Boolean true.

c.note: Requires <stdbool.h> (C99); 1 also works. C23 makes true a keyword.

haskell.note: Capitalized constructor.

lua.note: nil is the absence value.

c.note: Null pointer constant from <stddef.h>; C23 adds nullptr.

rust.note: No null; absence is Option::None (with Some(v) for present).

zig.note: Only valid for optional types (?T); not a general value.

julia.note: nothing is the unit/none value; missing exists separately for data.

haskell.note: No null; absence modeled by Maybe's Nothing constructor.

lua.note: Keyword and.

zig.note: Keyword, not && .

lua.note: Keyword or.

zig.note: Keyword, not || .

ruby.note: 'not' keyword also exists with lower precedence.

lua.note: Keyword not.

zig.note: ! for bool negation.

haskell.note: not is a function, not an operator.

ruby.note: No colons; terminated by 'end'; 'elsif' for chains.

php.note: Parens around condition; braces; 'elseif' for chains.

lua.note: then/else/end keywords; elseif for chaining.

rust.note: No parens around condition; braces required.

zig.note: Parens around condition required.

kotlin.note: if is an expression in Kotlin

scala.note: if is an expression; Scala 3 allows if cond then a else b

go.note: Braces mandatory; else on same line as closing brace.

haskell.code: if cond then x else y
haskell.note: else is mandatory (if is an expression).

ruby.note: Idiomatic iteration via .each; 'for x in xs' exists but is rare.

javascript.note: for...of iterates values; for...in is keys.

lua.code: for _, x in ipairs(xs) do end
lua.note: ipairs for arrays; pairs for all keys.

c.code: for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) { x = xs[i]; }
c.note: No native foreach; iterate by index over an array of length n.

cpp.note: Range-based for since C++11; use auto& to avoid copies.

rust.note: Iterates by IntoIterator; use &xs to borrow.

zig.note: Capture syntax |x|; for (xs, 0..) |x, i| to also get index.

go.note: Use i, x for index too.

haskell.note: From Control.Monad; or mapM_ f xs. Pure iteration is usually map/fold.

lua.note: Numeric for is inclusive; 0..n-1 mimics 0-based range.

rust.note: 0..n is exclusive of n; 0..=n is inclusive.

zig.note: Range form added in modern Zig; while loop also common.

go.note: Go's only loop keyword is for.

haskell.note: [0..n-1] is the range list.

lua.note: while/do/end.

go.note: No while keyword; for with single condition.

haskell.note: No native while; recursion is idiomatic, or Control.Monad.Loops.whileM. No single canonical spelling.

ruby.note: case/when uses === matching; case/in adds pattern matching (Ruby 3.0+).

php.note: switch falls through without break; match($x){1=>...} (PHP 8) is strict no-fallthrough.

javascript.note: switch; no pattern matching in JS.

lua.note: Lua has no switch/match; use if-elseif chain or a dispatch table.

c.note: switch on integer/enum only; no pattern matching.

cpp.note: Integral/enum switch only; no true pattern matching pre-C++26.

rust.note: match must be exhaustive; _ is the catch-all arm.

zig.note: Must be exhaustive; else is the catch-all prong.

csharp.note: or switch expression: x switch { 1 => a, _ => b }

swift.note: Must be exhaustive; supports pattern matching.

julia.note: No built-in switch; idiom is if/elseif or @match from Match.jl package.

haskell.note: Pattern matching via case ... of.

lua.note: Breaks innermost loop.

rust.note: Can break with a value from loop: break val;

zig.note: Labeled blocks can break with a value: break :blk val;

scala.note: no language break; needs scala.util.control.Breaks. Idiomatic code avoids it

haskell.note: No loops/break; control flow is recursion and higher-order functions.

lua.note: No continue keyword; idiom is goto continue with ::continue:: label before end (Lua 5.2+).

scala.note: no continue keyword; idiomatic code uses filter/recursion or guard

haskell.note: No loop continue; use guards/recursion.

python.code:
def f(a, b):
    return a + b

ruby.note: Last expression is the implicit return.

lua.note: function/end; local function for locals.

rust.note: Trailing expression (no ;) is the return value.

zig.note: Return type follows params with no arrow.

java.note: method; must live in a class

kotlin.note: or fun f(a: Int, b: Int) = a + b

scala.code: def f(a: Int, b: Int): Int = a + b

julia.note: Or one-line f(a, b) = a + b.

ruby.note: Explicit; usually omitted in favor of implicit last-expression return.

lua.note: return; must be last statement in a block.

rust.note: Often omitted in favor of trailing expression.

scala.note: explicit return discouraged; last expression is the value

haskell.note: Functions yield their body expression; there is no return statement. (return in do is monadic pure, not control flow.)

php.note: Arrow fn (PHP 7.4+) auto-captures by value; function($x){return $x;} is the closure form.

lua.note: Anonymous function expression; no arrow syntax.

c.note: Standard C has no closures/lambdas; only function pointers to named functions.

cpp.note: Lambda since C++11; [] is the capture list.

rust.note: Closure; |x| { ... } for a block body.

zig.note: No closures/lambdas; functions can't capture environment. Nested named/anonymous fns exist but capture nothing.

kotlin.note: single param can use implicit it

haskell.note: Backslash is lambda.

lua.note: Table with sequential integer keys (1-based).

c.note: Fixed-size array initializer; no built-in dynamic list.

rust.note: vec! macro builds a Vec; [1,2,3] is a fixed array.

zig.note: Fixed array; [_] infers length. Dynamic lists use std.ArrayList (needs an allocator).

python.note: dict literal; keys are objects (here a string).

ruby.note: { a: 1 } means key :a symbol; {"a" => 1} for string key.

php.note: Associative array; same array type as lists.

javascript.note: Object literal (string-keyed). Map for true map type.

typescript.note: Object literal; Record/Map for map types.

lua.note: Table with named keys; {["a"]=1} for string keys.

c.note: No built-in map/dict type or literal in standard C.

cpp.note: Requires <map>; std::unordered_map for a hash map.

rust.note: No map literal syntax; std::collections::HashMap::from over an array of pairs.

zig.note: No map literal; build std.AutoHashMap/StringHashMap at runtime with an allocator.

java.note: no key:value syntax; uses comma-separated pairs

haskell.note: From Data.Map; no literal dict syntax.

lua.note: Tables are 1-based; xs[1] is the first element.

rust.note: Panics on out-of-bounds; xs.get(0) returns Option.

julia.note: 1-indexed; xs[1] is first element. (xs[0] would error.)

haskell.note: !! is list indexing (O(n)).

php.note: count() for arrays; strlen() for strings.

lua.note: Length operator # on sequence tables/strings.

c.note: Works only for arrays in scope, not pointers; no general length op.

cpp.note: For std containers; std::size(xs) is generic (C++17).

zig.note: A field (not a call) on arrays/slices.

java.note: xs.length for arrays; xs.length() for String

csharp.note: xs.Length for arrays/strings; xs.Count() LINQ for IEnumerable

lua.note: Concatenation operator is .. (two dots).

c.note: In-place; a must have room. Use snprintf for safety; no + operator.

rust.note: Also a.to_string() + &b; format! is most general.

zig.note: ++ concatenates only at comptime (known-length arrays); runtime concat needs std.mem.concat with an allocator.

haskell.note: ++ concatenates lists (String = [Char]).

php.note: Inside double quotes; "hi {$name}" for complex expressions.

lua.note: No string interpolation; use string.format("hi %s", name) or .. concat. Lua 5.4 has no interp.

c.note: No string interpolation; use printf/snprintf format strings.

cpp.note: std::format since C++20 (<format>); no inline interpolation in the literal.

rust.note: Inline named captures in format strings since Rust 1.58.

zig.note: No string interpolation; use std.fmt with "{s}" placeholders, e.g. std.debug.print("hi {s}", .{name}).

java.note: no built-in interpolation in stable Java; use concatenation or String.format("hi %s", name)

go.note: No native interpolation syntax; use Sprintf.

haskell.note: No native interpolation in standard Haskell; use printf or Text.Printf, or the interpolate/string-interpolate package.

ruby.note: puts adds newline; print does not.

lua.note: print adds newline; io.write for no newline.

c.note: Requires <stdio.h>.

rust.note: Macro; appends newline. print! without newline.

zig.note: Goes to stderr actually; true stdout uses std.io.getStdOut().writer().print(...).

haskell.note: print adds quotes/uses Show; putStrLn for raw strings.

python.note: Reads one line, strips trailing newline; optional prompt arg.

ruby.note: Returns the line including trailing newline (or nil at EOF); gets.chomp to strip.

php.note: Returns line with trailing newline; rtrim() to strip. CLI SAPI.

javascript.note: Node has no sync readline builtin; readline module is canonical async way.

typescript.note: Same Node readline module as JS; no sync builtin.

lua.note: Reads a line from stdin; io.read() defaults to a line too ("*l" pre-5.3).

c.note: Reads up to size-1 chars including newline; <stdio.h>.

rust.note: line is a mutable String; keeps the trailing newline.

zig.note: Reads into a fixed buffer; readUntilDelimiterAlloc with an allocator for dynamic length.

csharp.note: returns string? (may be null at EOF)

haskell.note: getLine :: IO String.

php.note: require/include load files; 'use' imports namespaced symbols, not files.

javascript.note: ESM import; CommonJS: const m = require("m").

lua.note: require loads and caches a module.

c.note: Textual header inclusion via preprocessor; no module system pre-C23.

cpp.note: Classic include; C++20 modules use 'import m;'.

rust.note: use brings paths into scope; external crates declared in Cargo.toml.

zig.note: @import is a builtin returning the module's struct; std is @import("std").

java.note: imports a type/package, e.g. import java.util.List;

csharp.note: imports a namespace, e.g. using System;

julia.note: Or using m to bring exports into scope.

haskell.note: Module names are capitalized.

python.lex: CPython tokenizer (Parser/tokenizer.c) emits a token stream with INDENT/DEDENT tokens
python.parse: PEG parser builds a concrete then abstract syntax tree
python.ir: AST -> compiled to CPython bytecode (code objects, .pyc)
python.on_enter: CPython lexes and PEG-parses the source to an AST, compiles it to .pyc bytecode, then the CPython stack VM evaluates the bytecode.

ruby.lex: MRI/CRuby lexer (Ripper-style scanner in parse.y) produces tokens
ruby.parse: Bison-generated LALR parser builds an AST (node tree)
ruby.ir: AST compiled to YARV instruction sequences (ISeq) bytecode
ruby.runtime: YARV (Yet Another Ruby VM) in CRuby/MRI; optional YJIT for hot paths
ruby.on_enter: CRuby lexes and parses the source to an AST, compiles it into YARV ISeq bytecode, then the YARV stack VM executes it (optionally JIT-compiling hot methods via YJIT).

php.lex: Zend re2c-generated lexer (zend_language_scanner) tokenizes, splitting HTML vs PHP modes
php.parse: Bison LALR parser (zend_language_parser) builds an AST
php.runtime: Zend Engine VM; OPcache caches compiled opcodes; optional JIT (PHP 8+)
php.on_enter: The Zend Engine lexes and parses each request's script to an AST, compiles it to opcodes (cached by OPcache), then the Zend VM executes the opcodes, with optional JIT to native code in PHP 8+.

javascript.lex: Tokenizer scans UTF-16 source into tokens (handles ASI rules)
javascript.parse: Recursive-descent parser builds an ESTree-style AST
javascript.ir: AST then engine bytecode (e.g. V8 Ignition bytecode)
javascript.runtime: JS engine (V8, SpiderMonkey, JavaScriptCore) on Node.js or a browser
javascript.on_enter: The engine (e.g. V8) parses source to an AST, Ignition compiles it to bytecode and interprets it, and TurboFan JIT-compiles hot functions to native code.

typescript.lex: TypeScript compiler tokenizes source including type-annotation tokens
typescript.parse: tsc parser builds a TypeScript AST (superset of ESTree) with type nodes
typescript.ir: TS AST; type-checked then emitted as JavaScript (types erased)
typescript.runtime: None of its own; emitted JS runs on a JS engine (V8 etc.) via Node or browser
typescript.on_enter: tsc type-checks the TS AST then strips types and emits JavaScript, which a JS engine like V8 then JIT-executes (or ts-node/tsx transpiles on the fly).

lua.lex: Hand-written lexer scans source into tokens
lua.parse: Single-pass recursive-descent parser that emits bytecode directly (no persistent AST)
lua.runtime: The Lua virtual machine (lua_State); LuaJIT adds a tracing JIT
lua.on_enter: luac/the interpreter compiles source in one pass to register-based bytecode, then the Lua VM executes that bytecode (LuaJIT can trace and JIT hot paths to native code).

c.lex: hand-written lexer in the C compiler (e.g. clang/gcc) after the C preprocessor expands macros/includes
c.ir: compiler IR (clang -> LLVM IR; gcc -> GIMPLE/RTL)
c.runtime: none (links against libc; no managed runtime)
c.on_enter: The preprocessor expands directives, the compiler lexes/parses to an AST, lowers to IR (LLVM IR or GIMPLE), optimizes and emits a native object, then the linker produces a machine-code executable the OS runs directly.

cpp.lex: hand-written lexer in the C++ compiler after preprocessing
cpp.parse: recursive-descent parser (with template/overload handling) producing an AST
cpp.ir: compiler IR (clang -> LLVM IR; gcc -> GIMPLE/RTL)
cpp.on_enter: The preprocessor runs, the compiler parses to an AST, instantiates templates and resolves overloads, lowers to IR, optimizes and emits native object code, then the linker builds a native executable run directly by the OS.

rust.lex: rustc lexer tokenizes source (after macro-aware lexing)
rust.parse: recursive-descent parser -> AST, then macro expansion
rust.runtime: minimal (no GC/VM; small std runtime: panic handler, stack unwinding, allocator)
rust.on_enter: rustc lexes/parses to an AST, expands macros, lowers to HIR then MIR (borrow-checked there), emits LLVM IR which LLVM optimizes into a native binary linked with the small Rust std runtime and run directly.

zig.ir: AST -> ZIR -> AIR -> LLVM IR (or Zig self-hosted backend)
zig.on_enter: The Zig compiler tokenizes and parses to an AST, lowers to ZIR then per-function AIR (running comptime evaluation), emits machine code via the LLVM or self-hosted backend, and links a native executable run directly by the OS.

java.lex: javac scanner tokenizes .java source into Java tokens
java.parse: recursive-descent parser builds a compiler AST (com.sun.source Tree)
java.ir: JVM bytecode (.class files); HotSpot also uses internal C1/C2 IR + Sea-of-Nodes
java.on_enter: javac compiles .java to .class bytecode, then the JVM loads and interprets it, with HotSpot JIT-compiling hot methods to native code.

csharp.parse: Roslyn parser builds a syntax tree (red-green tree)
csharp.ir: CIL/MSIL bytecode in a managed assembly (.dll/.exe)
csharp.on_enter: Roslyn compiles C# to CIL bytecode in an assembly, then the CLR JIT-compiles CIL to native code at runtime (or AOT via ReadyToRun/NativeAOT).

kotlin.parse: parser builds a PSI/FIR tree (frontend IR)
kotlin.ir: JVM bytecode by default (also Kotlin/JS, Kotlin/Native LLVM IR)
kotlin.on_enter: kotlinc compiles .kt to JVM .class bytecode, then the JVM interprets and JIT-compiles it just like Java.

scala.parse: parser builds an AST; Scala 3 lowers through TASTy trees
scala.ir: JVM bytecode (.class); Scala 3 also emits TASTy; internal Tree/Type IR
scala.on_enter: scalac compiles .scala to JVM .class bytecode, then the JVM interprets and JIT-compiles it like other JVM languages.

go.lex: hand-written scanner (go/scanner) producing tokens with automatic semicolon insertion
go.runtime: Go runtime linked into the binary (goroutine scheduler, garbage collector); no separate VM
go.on_enter: The gc compiler lexes and parses each package to an AST, type-checks it, lowers to SSA IR, optimizes and emits machine code, then the linker produces a single static native executable embedding the Go runtime which the OS runs directly.

swift.lex: hand-written lexer in the Swift compiler frontend producing tokens
swift.ir: AST -> typechecked AST -> SIL (Swift Intermediate Language) -> LLVM IR
swift.runtime: Swift runtime library (ARC reference counting, metadata, dynamic casting); no managed VM
swift.on_enter: swiftc lexes and parses source to an AST, type-checks it, lowers to SIL for Swift-specific optimization, emits LLVM IR which LLVM compiles to a native object, and the linker produces a native binary linked against the Swift runtime.

julia.parse: parser (flisp, or JuliaSyntax.jl in recent versions) -> surface AST of Expr objects
julia.ir: lowered AST -> typed Julia IR (CodeInfo/SSA) -> LLVM IR -> native code
julia.runtime: Julia runtime with LLVM-based JIT and a garbage collector
julia.on_enter: Julia parses source to Expr ASTs, lowers and macro-expands them, then type-infers and JIT-compiles each method specialization through LLVM to native code on first call, executing under the Julia runtime/GC.

haskell.lex: GHC lexer with layout (indentation) algorithm inserting virtual braces/semicolons
haskell.ir: HsSyn -> Core (System FC) -> STG -> Cmm -> native or LLVM code (or bytecode in GHCi)
haskell.runtime: GHC RTS (lazy graph-reduction evaluator, garbage collector, green-thread scheduler)
haskell.on_enter: GHC lexes with layout handling and parses to an AST, type-checks and desugars to Core, optimizes Core, lowers through STG and Cmm to native machine code, and links a binary that runs under the GHC RTS performing lazy evaluation; GHCi instead interprets bytecode.
